findings.description: Bare `except:` clause silently swallows ALL exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit, MemoryError, and genuine programming errors. There is no logging, no re-raise, and no differentiation between transient network errors and fatal errors.
findings.evidence: except:\n    pass — all exception information is discarded; retry loop continues silently on any exception type

findings.description: All retry attempts fail silently. The only signal on exhaustion is a WARNING log at line 35, but no exception is raised and no structured error context (last exception, attempt count, URL) is captured. Callers receive `None` indistinguishably from 'no rows in feed'.
findings.evidence: Return value `None` is returned both when all retries fail and when the feed legitimately has no rows; caller at line 45 treats both as falsy and skips write

findings.description: `process_feed` writes to `output_dir` without any permission check or existence check. If the directory does not exist or is read-only, `write_text` raises an unhandled exception that propagates to the caller with no graceful degradation.
findings.evidence: output_path.write_text(json.dumps(valid_rows, indent=2)) — no directory existence check, no permission check, no try/except

findings.description: No tests exist for this module. There is no way to know if retry logic, failure degradation, or row filtering behaves correctly. The bare except makes it impossible for a test to observe that failures occurred.
findings.evidence: No test file referenced; bare except prevents exception propagation needed to assert failure behavior in tests

findings.description: `logging.basicConfig` is called at module import time (line 13). This is a side effect that configures the root logger for the entire process, which can silently override logging configuration set by the application that imports this module.
findings.evidence: logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, ...) at module top-level — any app importing this module after configuring its own logging will have its config silently overridden if basicConfig hasn't been called yet, or this call is silently ignored if it has

findings.description: `import urllib.request` and `import json` are placed inside functions rather than at the module top level, violating Python convention and hiding dependencies from module inspection tools and linters.
findings.evidence: import urllib.request inside fetch_partner_feed (line 21); import json inside process_feed (line 44) — both are stdlib modules with no conditional need

findings.description: `fetch_partner_feed` returns `None` on all failure paths and an empty list `[]` when the feed has no rows, but the docstring says 'Returns parsed rows on success, None on failure.' An empty list is ambiguous — it could be success-with-no-data or a masked parse failure.
findings.evidence: Return type Optional[List[Dict]] does not distinguish empty-feed from parse-failure; combined with bare except, parse errors silently yield None while an empty feed yields []

findings.description: The output file is always named `latest_feed.json` regardless of feed URL or timestamp. Concurrent calls to `process_feed` with different URLs will silently overwrite each other's output.
findings.evidence: output_path = Path(output_dir) / 'latest_feed.json' — fixed name, no locking, no atomicity

findings.description: No timeout or retry backoff between attempts. The retry loop immediately retries 3 times with no delay, which under network failure conditions will hammer the upstream endpoint and is unlikely to succeed where the previous attempt failed milliseconds earlier.
findings.evidence: for attempt in range(3): ... except: pass — no sleep/backoff between retries

findings.description: Row filtering at line 46 silently drops rows missing 'id' or 'timestamp' with no logging of how many rows were dropped. Data loss is invisible to operators.
findings.evidence: valid_rows = [r for r in rows if r.get('id') and r.get('timestamp')] — dropped rows not counted or logged

clean.location: lines 16, 39 — function signatures use type hints
lines 7-10 — only stdlib and typing imports at top level (urllib and json aside)
